The **Eucharist** ([/ˈjuːkərɪst/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*YOO-kər-ist*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); from [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek_language "Koine Greek language"): εὐχαριστία, romanized: *eucharistía*, lit. 'thanksgiving'), also called **Holy Communion**, the **Blessed Sacrament** or the **Lord's Supper**, is a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [rite](/wiki/Rite_(Christianity) "Rite (Christianity)"), considered a [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") in most churches and an [ordinance](/wiki/Ordinance_(Christianity) "Ordinance (Christianity)") in others. Christians believe that the rite was instituted by [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") at the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper"), the night before [his crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), giving his [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)") bread and wine. Passages in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") state that he commanded them to "do this in memory of me" while referring to the bread as "my body" and the cup of wine as "the blood of my covenant, which is poured out for many".[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) According to the [synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels"), this was at a [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") meal.[[3]](#cite_note-EB-3)

The elements of the Eucharist, [sacramental bread](/wiki/Sacramental_bread "Sacramental bread")—either [leavened](/wiki/Leavening_agent "Leavening agent") or [unleavened](/wiki/Unleavened_bread "Unleavened bread")—and [sacramental wine](/wiki/Sacramental_wine "Sacramental wine") (among [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans"), [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy")) or non-alcoholic grape juice (among [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") and [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren")), are consecrated on an [altar](/wiki/Altar "Altar") or a [communion table](/wiki/Communion_table "Communion table") and consumed thereafter. The consecrated elements are the end product of the [Eucharistic Prayer](/wiki/Anaphora_(liturgy) "Anaphora (liturgy)").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Christians generally recognize a special presence of Christ in this rite, though they differ about exactly how, where, and when Christ is present. The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") states that the Eucharist is the body and blood of Christ under the species of bread and wine. It maintains that by the consecration, the [substances](/wiki/Substance_theory "Substance theory") of the bread and wine actually become the substances of the body and blood of Christ ([transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation")) while the form and appearances of the bread and wine remain unaltered (e.g. colour, taste, feel, and smell). The [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") churches agree that an objective change occurs of the bread and wine into the body and blood of Christ. [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") believe the true body and blood of Christ are really present "in, with, and under" the forms of the bread and wine, known as the [sacramental union](/wiki/Sacramental_union "Sacramental union").[[5]](#cite_note-MattoxRoeber2012-5) [Reformed Christians](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") believe in a [real spiritual presence](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology") of Christ in the Eucharist.[[6]](#cite_note-McKim1998-6) Anglican [eucharistic theologies](/wiki/Eucharistic_theology "Eucharistic theology") universally affirm the [real presence of Christ in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist"), though [Evangelical Anglicans](/wiki/Evangelical_Anglicanism "Evangelical Anglicanism") believe that this is a spiritual presence, while [Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholic "Anglo-Catholic") hold to a [corporeal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/corporeal "wikt:corporeal") presence.[[7]](#cite_note-Poulson1999-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Others, such as the [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren"), hold the Lord's Supper to be a [memorial](/wiki/Memorialism "Memorialism") in which believers are "one with Him".[[9]](#cite_note-Bristow2007-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Gibson2022-10) As a result of these different understandings, "the Eucharist has been a central issue in the discussions and deliberations of the [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenical "Ecumenical") movement."[[3]](#cite_note-EB-3)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/%C3%9Altima_Cena_-_Juan_de_Juanes.jpg/250px-%C3%9Altima_Cena_-_Juan_de_Juanes.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%9Altima_Cena_-_Juan_de_Juanes.jpg)

The Eucharist has been a key theme in the depictions of the [Last Supper in Christian art](/wiki/Last_Supper_in_Christian_art "Last Supper in Christian art"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) as in this 16th-century [Juan de Juanes](/wiki/Vicente_Juan_Masip "Vicente Juan Masip") painting, after [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s *[Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")*.

### Eucharist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Eucharist")]

The [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") was originally written in the [Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") language and the Greek noun εὐχαριστία (*eucharistia*), meaning "thanksgiving", appears a few times in it,[[12]](#cite_note-12) while the related Greek verb εὐχαριστήσας is found several times in New Testament accounts of the Last Supper,[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-LaV-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Schr-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) including the earliest such account:[[14]](#cite_note-LaV-14)

> For I received from the Lord what I also delivered to you, that the Lord Jesus on the night when he was betrayed took bread, and when he had given thanks (εὐχαριστήσας), he broke it, and said, "This is my body which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me".

— 1 Corinthians 11:23–24[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The term *eucharistia* (thanksgiving) is that by which the rite is referred to[[14]](#cite_note-LaV-14) in the *[Didache](/wiki/Didache "Didache")* (a late 1st or early 2nd century document),[[19]](#cite_note-Kodell-19): 51[[20]](#cite_note-textDid-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Bromiley437-21): 437[[22]](#cite_note-22): 207 by [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch") (who died between 98 and 117)[[21]](#cite_note-Bromiley437-21)[[23]](#cite_note-23) and by [Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") (*[First Apology](/wiki/First_Apology_of_Justin_Martyr "First Apology of Justin Martyr")* written between 155 and 157).[[24]](#cite_note-Guy196-24)[[21]](#cite_note-Bromiley437-21)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Today, "the Eucharist" is the name still used by [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox"), [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox"), [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans"), [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterians "Presbyterians"), and [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"). Other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") denominations rarely use this term, preferring "Communion", "the Lord's Supper", "Remembrance", or "the Breaking of Bread". [Latter-day Saints](/wiki/Latter-day_Saints "Latter-day Saints") call it "[the Sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament_(LDS_Church) "Sacrament (LDS Church)")".[[26]](#cite_note-LDS_Sacrament-26)

### Lord's Supper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Lord's Supper")]

In the [First Epistle to the Corinthians](/wiki/First_Epistle_to_the_Corinthians "First Epistle to the Corinthians") Paul uses the term "Lord's Supper", in Greek Κυριακὸν δεῖπνον (*Kyriakon deipnon*), in the early 50s of the 1st century:[[14]](#cite_note-LaV-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Schr-15)

> When you come together, it is not the Lord's Supper you eat, for as you eat, each of you goes ahead without waiting for anybody else. One remains hungry, another gets drunk.

— 1 Corinthians 11:20–21[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The term "Lord's Supper" came into popular use after the Protestant Reformation and remains the predominant term among certain [Evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), such as [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") and [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostals "Pentecostals").[[28]](#cite_note-28): 123[[29]](#cite_note-29): 259[[30]](#cite_note-30): 371

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Last-supper-from-Kremikovtsi.jpg/250px-Last-supper-from-Kremikovtsi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Last-supper-from-Kremikovtsi.jpg)

A [Kremikovtsi Monastery](/wiki/Kremikovtsi_Monastery "Kremikovtsi Monastery") fresco (15th century) depicting the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") celebrated by Jesus and his disciples. The early Christians would also have celebrated this meal to commemorate Jesus' death and subsequent resurrection.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Cathedral_Fribourg_vitrail_Eucharistie_01.jpg/250px-Cathedral_Fribourg_vitrail_Eucharistie_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathedral_Fribourg_vitrail_Eucharistie_01.jpg)

Eucharistic window (1898–1900) by [Józef Mehoffer](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef_Mehoffer "Józef Mehoffer")

### Communion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Communion")]

Use of the term *Communion* (or *Holy Communion*) to refer to the Eucharistic rite began with some[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] groups originating in the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). Others, such as the Catholic Church, do not formally use this term for the rite, but instead mean by it the act of partaking of the consecrated elements;[[31]](#cite_note-31) they speak of receiving Holy Communion at Mass or outside of it, they also use the term [First Communion](/wiki/First_Communion "First Communion") when one receives the Eucharist for the first time. The term *Communion* is derived from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") *communio* ("sharing in common"), translated from the Greek κοινωνία (*koinōnía*) in 1 Corinthians 10:16:

> The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the *communion* of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the *communion* of the body of Christ?

— 1 Corinthians 10:16

### Other terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other terms")]

#### Breaking of bread

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Breaking of bread")]

The phrase κλάσις τοῦ ἄρτου (*klasis tou artou*, 'breaking of the bread'; in later liturgical Greek also ἀρτοκλασία *artoklasia*) appears in various related forms five times in the New Testament[[32]](#cite_note-32) in contexts which, according to some, may refer to the celebration of the Eucharist, in either closer or symbolically more distant reference to the Last Supper.[[33]](#cite_note-33) This term is used by the [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

#### Sacrament or Blessed Sacrament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sacrament or Blessed Sacrament")]

The "[Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament")", the "Sacrament of the Altar", and other variations, are common terms used by Catholics,[[35]](#cite_note-35) Lutherans[[36]](#cite_note-36) and some Anglicans ([Anglo-Catholics](/wiki/Anglo-Catholics "Anglo-Catholics"))[[37]](#cite_note-37) for the consecrated elements, particularly when [reserved](/wiki/Reserved_sacrament "Reserved sacrament") in a [tabernacle](/wiki/Church_tabernacle "Church tabernacle"). In [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") the term "[The Sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament_(Mormonism) "Sacrament (Mormonism)")" is used of the rite.[[26]](#cite_note-LDS_Sacrament-26)

#### Mass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mass")]

Main articles: [Mass (liturgy)](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") and [Mass in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Mass_in_the_Catholic_Church "Mass in the Catholic Church")

The term "[Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)")" is used in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") churches (especially the [Churches of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"), [Norway](/wiki/Church_of_Norway "Church of Norway") and [Finland](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Finland "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Finland")), and by some Anglicans. It derives from the Latin word *missa*, a dismissal: *"[Ite missa est](/wiki/Ite_missa_est "Ite missa est")",* or "go, it is sent", the very last phrase of the service.[[38]](#cite_note-38) That Latin word has come to imply "mission" as well because the congregation is sent out to serve Christ.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

At least in the Catholic Church, the Mass is a long rite in two parts: the [Liturgy of the Word](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Word "Liturgy of the Word") and the [Liturgy of the Eucharist](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Eucharist "Liturgy of the Eucharist"). The former consists of [readings](/wiki/Lection "Lection") from the Bible and a [homily](/wiki/Homily "Homily"), or sermon, given by a priest or deacon. The latter, which follows seamlessly, includes the "[Offering](/wiki/Offertory "Offertory")" of the bread and wine at the altar, their consecration by the priest through prayer, and their reception by the congregation in Holy Communion.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Among the many other terms used in the Catholic Church are "Holy Mass", "the Memorial of the Passion, Death and Resurrection of the Lord", the "Holy Sacrifice of the Mass", and the "Holy Mysteries".[[41]](#cite_note-41)

#### Divine Liturgy and Divine Service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Divine Liturgy and Divine Service")]

The term [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Θεία Λειτουργία) is used in [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") traditions, whether in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") or among the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"). These also speak of "the Divine Mysteries", especially in reference to the consecrated elements, which they also call "the Holy Gifts".[[a]](#cite_note-42)

The term [Divine Service](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Lutheran) "Divine Service (Lutheran)") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Gottesdienst*) has often been used to refer to Christian worship more generally and is still used in [Lutheran churches](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), in addition to the terms "Eucharist", "Mass" and "Holy Communion".[[42]](#cite_note-Spicer2016-43) Historically this refers (like the term "worship" itself) to service of God, although more recently it has been associated with the idea that God is serving the congregants in the liturgy.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

#### Other Eastern rites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Other Eastern rites")]

Some Eastern rites have yet more names for the Eucharist. [Holy Qurbana](/wiki/Holy_Qurbana "Holy Qurbana") is common in [Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity") and *Badarak*[[44]](#cite_note-45) in the [Armenian Rite](/wiki/Armenian_Rite "Armenian Rite"); in the [Alexandrian Rite](/wiki/Alexandrian_Rite "Alexandrian Rite"), the term *[prosphora](/wiki/Prosphora "Prosphora")* (from the Greek προσφορά) is common in [Coptic Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Egypt "Christianity in Egypt") and *Keddase* in [Ethiopian](/wiki/Ethiopian_Christianity "Ethiopian Christianity") and [Eritrean Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Eritrea "Christianity in Eritrea").[[45]](#cite_note-Bradshaw2012-46)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Origin of the Eucharist](/wiki/Origin_of_the_Eucharist "Origin of the Eucharist")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Juan_de_Juanes_002.jpg/250px-Juan_de_Juanes_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juan_de_Juanes_002.jpg)

*Christ with the Eucharist*, [Vicente Juan Masip](/wiki/Vicente_Juan_Masip "Vicente Juan Masip"), 16th century

### Biblical basis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Biblical basis")]

The [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") appears in all three [synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels"): [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_according_to_Matthew "Gospel according to Matthew"), [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_according_to_Mark "Gospel according to Mark"), and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_according_to_Luke "Gospel according to Luke"). It also is found in the [First Epistle to the Corinthians](/wiki/First_Epistle_to_the_Corinthians "First Epistle to the Corinthians"),[[3]](#cite_note-EB-3)[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48) which suggests how early Christians celebrated what [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") called the Lord's Supper. Although the [Gospel of John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") does not reference the Last Supper explicitly, some argue that it contains theological allusions to the early Christian celebration of the Eucharist, especially in the chapter 6 [Bread of Life Discourse](/wiki/Bread_of_Life_Discourse "Bread of Life Discourse") but also in other passages.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

#### Gospels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Gospels")]

The synoptic Gospels, Mark 14:22–25,[[49]](#cite_note-50) Matthew 26:26–29[[50]](#cite_note-51) and Luke 22:13–20[[51]](#cite_note-52) depict Jesus as presiding over the Last Supper prior to his crucifixion. The versions in Matthew and Mark are almost identical,[[52]](#cite_note-53) but the Gospel of Luke presents a textual difference, in that a few manuscripts omit the second half of verse 19 and all of verse 20 ("given for you […] poured out for you"), which are found in the vast majority of ancient witnesses to the text.[[53]](#cite_note-54) If the shorter text is the original one, then Luke's account is independent of both that of Paul and that of Matthew/Mark. If the majority longer text comes from the author of the third gospel, then this version is very similar to that of Paul in 1 Corinthians, being somewhat fuller in its description of the early part of the Supper,[[54]](#cite_note-55) particularly in making specific mention of a cup being blessed before the bread was broken.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In the one prayer given to posterity by Jesus, the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s_Prayer "Lord's Prayer"), the word [epiousion](/wiki/Epiousion "Epiousion")—which is otherwise unknown in Classical Greek literature—was interpreted by some early Christian writers as meaning "super-substantial", and hence a possible reference to the Eucharist as the [Bread of Life](/wiki/Bread_of_Life "Bread of Life").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

In the Gospel of John, however, the account of the Last Supper does not mention Jesus taking bread and "the cup" and speaking of them as his body and blood; instead, it recounts other events: his humble act of washing the disciples' feet, the prophecy of the betrayal, which set in motion the events that would lead to the cross, and his long discourse in response to some questions posed by his followers, in which he went on to speak of the importance of the unity of the disciples with him, with each other, and with God.[[57]](#cite_note-Harris-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59) Some would find in this unity and in the washing of the feet the deeper meaning of the Communion bread in the other three Gospels.[[59]](#cite_note-60) In John 6:26–65,[[60]](#cite_note-61) a long discourse is attributed to Jesus that deals with the subject of the living bread; John 6:51–59[[61]](#cite_note-62) also contains echoes of Eucharistic language.

#### First Epistle to the Corinthians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: First Epistle to the Corinthians")]

1 Corinthians 11:23–25[[62]](#cite_note-63) gives the earliest recorded description of Jesus' Last Supper: "The Lord Jesus on the night when he was betrayed took bread, and when he had given thanks, he broke it and said, 'This is my body, which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' In the same way he took the cup also, after supper, saying, 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of me.”" The Greek word used in the passage for 'remembrance' is ἀνάμνησιν (*[anamnesis](/wiki/Anamnesis_(Christianity) "Anamnesis (Christianity)")*), which itself has a much richer theological history than the English word "remember".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Agape_feast_07.jpg/250px-Agape_feast_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agape_feast_07.jpg)

Early Christian painting of an Agape feast

The expression "The Lord's Supper", derived from [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle")'s usage in 1 Corinthians 11:17–34,[[63]](#cite_note-64) may have originally referred to the [Agape feast](/wiki/Agape_feast "Agape feast") (or love feast), the shared [communal meal](/wiki/Communal_meal "Communal meal") with which the Eucharist was originally associated.[[64]](#cite_note-65) The Agape feast is mentioned in Jude 12[[65]](#cite_note-66) but "The Lord's Supper" is now commonly used in reference to a celebration involving no food other than the sacramental bread and wine.

### Early Christian sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Early Christian sources")]

The *[Didache](/wiki/Didache "Didache")* (Greek: Διδαχή, "teaching") is an [Early Church](/wiki/Early_Church "Early Church") treatise that includes instructions for [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") and the Eucharist. Most scholars date it to the late 1st century,[[66]](#cite_note-67) and distinguish in it two separate Eucharistic traditions, the earlier tradition in chapter 10 and the later one preceding it in chapter 9.[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[b]](#cite_note-69) The Eucharist is mentioned again in chapter 14.[[c]](#cite_note-70)

[Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch") (born c. 35 or 50, died between 98 and 117), one of the Apostolic Fathers,[[d]](#cite_note-72) mentions the Eucharist as "the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ":

> They abstain from the Eucharist and from prayer, because they confess not the Eucharist to be the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ, which suffered for our sins, and which the Father, of His goodness, raised up again. [...] Let that be deemed a proper Eucharist, which is [administered] either by the bishop, or by one to whom he has entrusted it.

— Smyrnaeans, 7–8[[69]](#cite_note-73)

> Take heed, then, to have but one Eucharist. For there is one flesh of our Lord Jesus Christ, and one cup to [show forth ] the unity of His blood; one altar; as there is one bishop, along with the presbytery and deacons, my fellow-servants: that so, whatsoever you do, you may do it according to [the will of] God.

— Philadephians, 4[[70]](#cite_note-74)

In recent scholarship on Ignatius, however, it has been argued that Ignatius uses the term 'Eucharist' to denote the ecclesial gathering, and not the eucharistic elements per se.[[71]](#cite_note-75) But even if sacramental realism can no longer be taken from his letter to the Smyrnaeans, it is plausibly expressed in the martyrological language of Romans 7.[[72]](#cite_note-76)

[Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") (born c. 100, died c. 165) mentions in this regard:

> And this food is called among us Εὐχαριστία [the Eucharist], of which no one is allowed to partake but the man who believes that the things which we teach are true, and who has been washed with the washing that is for the remission of sins, and unto regeneration, and who is so living as Christ has enjoined. For not as common bread and common drink do we receive these; but in like manner as Jesus Christ our Saviour, having been made flesh by the Word of God, had both flesh and blood for our salvation, so likewise have we been taught that the food which is blessed by the prayer of His word, and from which our blood and flesh by transmutation are nourished, is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh.[[73]](#cite_note-77)[[74]](#cite_note-78)

[Paschasius Radbertus](/wiki/Paschasius_Radbertus "Paschasius Radbertus") (785–865) was a Carolingian theologian, and the abbot of [Corbie](/wiki/Corbie_Abbey "Corbie Abbey"), whose best-known and influential work is an exposition on the nature of the Eucharist written around 831, entitled *De Corpore et Sanguine Domini*. In it, Paschasius agrees with [St Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") in affirming that the Eucharist contains the true, historical body of Jesus Christ. According to Radbertus, God is truth itself, and therefore, his words and actions must be true. Christ's proclamation at the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") that the bread and wine were his body and blood must be taken literally, since God is truth.[[75]](#cite_note-Chazelle-79): 9 He thus believes that the [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") of the bread and wine offered in the Eucharist really occurs. Only if the Eucharist is the actual body and blood of Christ can a Christian know it is salvific.[[75]](#cite_note-Chazelle-79): 10[[e]](#cite_note-80)

### Jews and the Eucharist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Jews and the Eucharist")]

The concept of the Jews both destroying and partaking in some perverted version of the Eucharist has been a vessel to promote [anti-Judaism](/wiki/Anti-Judaism "Anti-Judaism") and anti-Jewish ideology and violence. In medieval times, Jews were often depicted stabbing or in some other way physically harming communion wafers; this was known as [host desecration](/wiki/Host_desecration "Host desecration").[[76]](#cite_note-:1-81) These characterizations drew parallels to the idea that the Jews killed Christ; murdering this transubstantiation or "host" was thought of as a repetition of the event. Jewish people's eagerness to destroy hosts were also a variation of [blood libel](/wiki/Blood_libel "Blood libel") charges, with Jews being accused of murdering bodies of Christ, whether they be communion wafers or Christian children. The blood libel charges and the concept of Eucharist are also related in the belief that blood is efficacious, meaning it has some sort of divine power.[[77]](#cite_note-82)

## Eucharistic theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Eucharistic theology")]

Main article: [Eucharistic theology](/wiki/Eucharistic_theology "Eucharistic theology")

Most Christians, even those who deny that there is any real change in the elements used, recognize a special presence of [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") in this rite. However, Christians differ about exactly how, where and how long Christ is present in it.[[3]](#cite_note-EB-3) [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), and the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") teach that the reality (the "substance") of the elements of bread and wine is wholly changed into the body and blood of Jesus Christ, while the appearances (the "species") remain. [Transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") ("change of the substance") is the term used by Catholics to denote *what* is changed, not to explain *how* the change occurs, since the Catholic Church teaches that "the signs of bread and wine become, *in a way surpassing understanding*, the Body and Blood of Christ".[[78]](#cite_note-83) The Orthodox use various terms such as transelementation, but no explanation is official as they prefer to leave it a mystery.

[Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") believe Christ to be "truly and substantially present" with the bread and wine that are seen in the Eucharist,[[79]](#cite_note-84) in a manner referred to as the [sacramental union](/wiki/Sacramental_union "Sacramental union"). They attribute the real presence of Jesus' living body to his word spoken in the Eucharist, and not to the faith of those receiving it. They also believe that "forgiveness of sins, life, and salvation" are given through the words of Christ in the Eucharist to those who believe his words ("given and shed for you").[[80]](#cite_note-85)

[Reformed Christians](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") also believe Christ to be present in the Eucharist, but describe this presence as a [spiritual presence](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology"), not a physical one.[[81]](#cite_note-86) Anglicans adhere to [a range of views](/wiki/Anglican_eucharistic_theology "Anglican eucharistic theology") depending on [churchmanship](/wiki/Churchmanship "Churchmanship") although the teaching in the Anglican [Thirty-Nine Articles](/wiki/Thirty-Nine_Articles "Thirty-Nine Articles") also holds that the body of Christ is received by the faithful only in a heavenly and spiritual manner, a doctrine also taught in the Methodist [Articles of Religion](/wiki/Articles_of_Religion_(Methodist) "Articles of Religion (Methodist)").[[82]](#cite_note-Strout2024-87)

Christians adhering to the theology of [Memorialism](/wiki/Memorialism "Memorialism"), such as the [Anabaptist Churches](/wiki/Anabaptist_Church "Anabaptist Church"), do not believe in the concept of the real presence, believing that the Eucharist is only a ceremonial remembrance or [memorial](/wiki/Memorialism "Memorialism") of the death of Christ.[[83]](#cite_note-Finger2010-88)

The *Baptism, Eucharist and Ministry* document of the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches"),[[84]](#cite_note-89) attempting to present the common understanding of the Eucharist on the part of the generality of Christians, describes it as "essentially the sacrament of the gift which God makes to us in Christ through the power of the Holy Spirit", "Thanksgiving to the Father", "Anamnesis or Memorial of Christ", "the sacrament of the unique sacrifice of Christ, who ever lives to make intercession for us", "the sacrament of the body and blood of Christ, the sacrament of his [real presence](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist")", "Invocation of the Spirit", "Communion of the Faithful", and "Meal of the Kingdom".

## Ritual and liturgy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Ritual and liturgy")]

Many Christian denominations classify the Eucharist as a [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament").[[f]](#cite_note-91) Anabaptists usually refer to the Lord's Supper as being an *[ordinance](/wiki/Ordinance_(Christianity) "Ordinance (Christianity)")*, viewing it as an expression of faith and of obedience to Christ, though Anabaptists have used the word *sacrament* interchangeably with the word *ordinance*.[[86]](#cite_note-Kauffman1898-92)

### Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

Main article: [Eucharist in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Eucharist_in_the_Catholic_Church "Eucharist in the Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Ecce_Agnus_Dei.jpg/250px-Ecce_Agnus_Dei.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ecce_Agnus_Dei.jpg)

At a Solemn [Tridentine Mass](/wiki/Tridentine_Mass "Tridentine Mass"), the Host is displayed to the people before Communion.

In the Catholic Church the Eucharist is considered as a [sacrament](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church"), according to the church the Eucharist is "the source and summit of the Christian life".[[87]](#cite_note-93)[[88]](#cite_note-94) "The other sacraments, and indeed all ecclesiastical ministries and works of the apostolate, are bound up with the Eucharist and are oriented toward it. For in the blessed Eucharist is contained the whole spiritual good of the Church, namely Christ himself, our Pasch."[[89]](#cite_note-95) ("Pasch" is a word that sometimes means Easter, sometimes Passover.)[[90]](#cite_note-96)

#### As a sacrifice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: As a sacrifice")]

Main article: [Eucharist in the Catholic Church § Eucharist in the Mass](/wiki/Eucharist_in_the_Catholic_Church#Eucharist_in_the_Mass "Eucharist in the Catholic Church")

In the Eucharist the same sacrifice that Jesus made only once on the cross is believed to be made present at every Mass. According to [Compendium of the Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Compendium_of_the_Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Compendium of the Catechism of the Catholic Church"), "The Eucharist is the very sacrifice of the Body and Blood of the Lord Jesus which he instituted to perpetuate the sacrifice of the cross throughout the ages until his return in glory."[[91]](#cite_note-97)

"When the Church celebrates the Eucharist, she commemorates Christ's Passover, and it is made present the sacrifice Christ offered once for all on the cross remains ever present. [...] The Eucharist is thus a sacrifice because it re-presents (makes present) the same and only sacrifice offered once for all on the cross"[[92]](#cite_note-98)

The sacrifice of Christ and the sacrifice of the Eucharist are considered as one single sacrifice: "The victim is one and the same: the same now offers through the ministry of priests, who then offered himself on the cross; only the manner of offering is different."[[93]](#cite_note-scborromeo.org-99) In the holy sacrifice of the Mass, "it is Christ himself, the eternal high priest of the New Covenant who, acting through the ministry of the priests, offers the Eucharistic sacrifice. And it is the same Christ, really present under the species of bread and wine, who is the offering of the Eucharistic sacrifice."[[94]](#cite_note-100)

#### As a real presence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: As a real presence")]

Main article: [Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/20190529_Spain_and_Portugal_El_Camino_Pilgrimage_1063_%2848002601588%29.jpg/250px-20190529_Spain_and_Portugal_El_Camino_Pilgrimage_1063_%2848002601588%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20190529_Spain_and_Portugal_El_Camino_Pilgrimage_1063_(48002601588).jpg)

Eucharistic celebration at the [Sanctuary of Our Lady of Fátima](/wiki/Sanctuary_of_F%C3%A1tima "Sanctuary of Fátima")

According to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") Jesus Christ is present in the Eucharist in a true, real and substantial way, with his body, blood, soul and divinity.[[95]](#cite_note-101) By the [consecration](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration"), the [substances](/wiki/Substance_theory "Substance theory") of the bread and wine actually become the substances of the body and blood of Christ ([transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation")) while the appearances or "species" of the bread and wine remain unaltered (e.g. colour, taste, feel, and smell). This change is brought about in the eucharistic prayer through the efficacy of the word of Christ and by the action of the Holy Spirit.[[96]](#cite_note-102)[[97]](#cite_note-103)[[98]](#cite_note-104) The Eucharistic presence of Christ begins at the moment of the consecration and endures as long as the Eucharistic species subsist,[[99]](#cite_note-105)[[100]](#cite_note-106) that is, until the Eucharist is digested, physically destroyed, or decays by some natural process[[101]](#cite_note-107) (at which point, theologian [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") argued, the substance of the bread and wine cannot return).[[102]](#cite_note-108)

The [Fourth Council of the Lateran](/wiki/Fourth_Council_of_the_Lateran "Fourth Council of the Lateran") in 1215 spoke of the bread and wine as "transubstantiated" into the body and blood of Christ: "His body and blood are truly contained in the sacrament of the altar under the forms of bread and wine, the bread and wine having been transubstantiated, by God's power, into his body and blood".[[g]](#cite_note-111)[[105]](#cite_note-112) In 1551, the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") definitively declared: "Because Christ our Redeemer said that it was truly his body that he was offering under the species of bread,[[106]](#cite_note-113) it has always been the conviction of the Church of God, and this holy Council now declares again that by the consecration of the bread and wine there takes place a change of the whole substance of the bread into the substance of the body of Christ and of the whole substance of the wine into the substance of his blood. This change the holy Catholic Church has fittingly and properly called [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation")."[[107]](#cite_note-114)[[108]](#cite_note-115)[[109]](#cite_note-116)

The church holds that the body and blood of Jesus can no longer be truly separated. Where one is, the other must be. Therefore, although the priest (or [extraordinary minister of Holy Communion](/wiki/Extraordinary_minister_of_Holy_Communion "Extraordinary minister of Holy Communion")) says "The Body of Christ" when administering the Host and "The Blood of Christ" when presenting the chalice, the communicant who receives either one receives Christ, whole and entire. "Christ is present whole and entire in each of the species and whole and entire in each of their parts, in such a way that the breaking of the bread does not divide Christ."[[110]](#cite_note-117)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/BentoXVI-51-11052007_%28frag%29.jpg/250px-BentoXVI-51-11052007_%28frag%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:BentoXVI-51-11052007_(frag).jpg)

[Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") celebrates a Mass.

The Catholic Church sees as the main basis for this belief the words of Jesus himself at his Last Supper: the synoptic Gospels[[111]](#cite_note-118) and Paul's recount that Jesus at the time of taking the bread and the cup said: "This is my body […] this is my blood."[[112]](#cite_note-119) The Catholic understanding of these words, from the [Patristic](/wiki/Patristics "Patristics") authors onward, has emphasized their roots in the covenantal history of the Old Testament. The interpretation of Christ's words against this Old Testament background coheres with and supports belief in the [Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist").[[113]](#cite_note-120)

### Reception and devotions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Reception and devotions")]

According to the Catholic Church doctrine receiving the Eucharist in a state of [mortal sin](/wiki/Mortal_sin "Mortal sin") is a [sacrilege](/wiki/Sacrilege "Sacrilege")[[114]](#cite_note-121) and only those who are in a state of grace, that is, without any mortal sin, can receive it.[[115]](#cite_note-122) Based on 1 Corinthians 11:27–29, it affirms the following: "Anyone who is aware of having committed a mortal sin must not receive Holy Communion, even if he experiences deep contrition, without having first received [sacramental absolution](/wiki/Penance_(Catholic_Church) "Penance (Catholic Church)"), unless he has a grave reason for receiving Communion and there is no possibility of going to confession."[[116]](#cite_note-123)[[117]](#cite_note-124)

Since the Eucharist is the body and blood of Christ, "the worship due to the sacrament of the Eucharist, whether during the celebration of the Mass or outside it, is the worship of *latria*, that is, the adoration given to God alone.""[[118]](#cite_note-125) The Blessed Sacrament can be exposed (displayed) on an [altar](/wiki/Altar "Altar") in a [monstrance](/wiki/Monstrance "Monstrance"). Rites involving the exposure of the Blessed Sacrament include [Benediction](/wiki/Benediction "Benediction") and [eucharistic adoration](/wiki/Eucharistic_adoration "Eucharistic adoration"). According to [Catholic theology](/wiki/Catholic_theology "Catholic theology"), the host, after the Rite of Consecration, is no longer bread, but is the Body, Blood, Soul, and Divinity of Christ. Catholics believe that Jesus is the sacrificial [Lamb of God](/wiki/Lamb_of_God "Lamb of God") prefigured in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover"). The flesh of that Passover sacrificial lamb was to be consumed by the family members. Any left overs were to be burned before daybreak so that none of the Passover Lamb's flesh remained. Only by marking the doorposts and lintel of one's home with the Blood of the Lamb were the members of the household saved from death. The consumption of the Lamb was not to save them but rather to give them energy for the journey of escape (Exodus = escape from slavery in Egypt) as was also true for the unleavened bread ([Exodus 12:3–13](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Exodus%2012:3–13&version=nrsv)) As the Passover was the [Old Covenant](/wiki/Old_Covenant "Old Covenant"), so the Eucharist became the [New Covenant](/wiki/New_Covenant "New Covenant"). ([Matthew 26:26–28](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%2026:26–28&version=nrsv), [Mark 14:22–24](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Mark%2014:22–24&version=nrsv), [Luke 22: 19–20](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Luke%2022:19–20&version=nrsv), and [John 6:48–58](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=John%206:48–58&version=nrsv))

### Eastern Orthodoxy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodoxy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Liturgy_St_James_1.jpg/250px-Liturgy_St_James_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liturgy_St_James_1.jpg)

Eucharistic elements prepared for the Divine Liturgy

Main article: [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy")

Within [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), the Eucharistic service is called the "Divine Liturgy" ([Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite")) or similar names in other rites. It comprises two main divisions: the first is the "Liturgy of the Catechumens" which consists of introductory litanies, antiphons and scripture readings, culminating in a reading from one of the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospels "Gospels") and, often, a [homily](/wiki/Homily "Homily"); the second is the "Liturgy of the Faithful" in which the Eucharist is offered, consecrated, and received as Holy Communion. Within the latter, the actual Eucharistic prayer is called the *[anaphora](/wiki/Anaphora_(liturgy) "Anaphora (liturgy)")*, (literally "offering" or "carrying up", from the Greek ἀνα- + φέρω). In the [Rite of Constantinople](/wiki/Rite_of_Constantinople "Rite of Constantinople"), two different anaphoras are currently used: one is attributed to [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom"), the other to [Basil the Great](/wiki/Basil_of_Caesarea "Basil of Caesarea").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Praying_Before_the_Communion_at_Megara_%28Cleaner%29.png/250px-Praying_Before_the_Communion_at_Megara_%28Cleaner%29.png)](/wiki/File:Praying_Before_the_Communion_at_Megara_(Cleaner).png)

*Praying Before the Communion at Megara* by [Théodore Jacques Ralli](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_Ralli "Théodore Ralli") (1890) shows Greek Orthodox women reciting The Kanon of Preparation to Receive Holy Communion

In the [Oriental Orthodox Church](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), a variety of anaphoras are used, but all are similar in structure to those of the Constantinopolitan Rite, in which the Anaphora of Saint John Chrysostom is used most days of the year; Saint Basil's is offered on the Sundays of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"), the eves of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and [Theophany](/wiki/Theophany "Theophany"), [Holy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"), [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday"), and upon his feast day (1 January). At the conclusion of the Anaphora, the bread and wine are held to be the body and blood of Christ. Unlike the Latin Church, the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") uses leavened bread, with the leaven symbolizing the presence of the Holy Spirit.[[119]](#cite_note-126) The [Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") utilizes leavened bread in their celebration and requires parishioners to recite *The Kanon of Preparation to Receive Holy Communion* before receiving communion.[[120]](#cite_note-127)

In Eastern theology, one idea of consecration as a process has been suggested. This understands the change in the elements to be accomplished at the [epiclesis](/wiki/Epiclesis "Epiclesis") ("invocation") by which the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") is invoked and the [consecration](/wiki/Consecration_in_Eastern_Christianity "Consecration in Eastern Christianity") of the bread and wine as the genuine body and blood of Christ is specifically requested, but since the anaphora as a whole is considered a unitary (albeit lengthy) prayer, no one moment within it can readily be singled out.[[121]](#cite_note-128)

### Protestantism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Protestantism")]

#### Anabaptists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Anabaptists")]

Further information: [Anabaptist theology § Lord's Supper](/wiki/Anabaptist_theology#Lord's_Supper "Anabaptist theology")

Anabaptist denominations, such as the [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonite "Mennonite") and German Baptist Brethren Churches like the [Church of the Brethren](/wiki/Church_of_the_Brethren "Church of the Brethren") churches and congregations have the [Agape feast](/wiki/Lovefeast "Lovefeast"), [footwashing](/wiki/Footwashing "Footwashing"), as well as the serving of the bread and wine in the celebration of the [Lovefeast](/wiki/Lovefeast "Lovefeast"). In the more modern groups, Communion is only the serving of the Lord's Supper. In the communion meal, the members of the Mennonite churches renew their covenant with God and with each other.[[122]](#cite_note-129)

#### Moravian/Hussite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Moravian/Hussite")]

The [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") adheres to a view known as the "sacramental presence",[[123]](#cite_note-Atwood2010-130) teaching that in the [sacrament](/wiki/Sacrament "Sacrament") of [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion"):[[124]](#cite_note-VelikoGros2005-131)

> Christ gives his body and blood according to his promise to all who partake of the elements. When we eat and drink the bread and the wine of the Supper with expectant faith, we thereby have communion with the body and blood of our Lord and receive the forgiveness of sins, life, and salvation. In this sense, the bread and wine are rightly said to be Christ's body and blood which he gives to his disciples.[[124]](#cite_note-VelikoGros2005-131)

[Nicolaus Zinzendorf](/wiki/Nicolaus_Zinzendorf "Nicolaus Zinzendorf"), a [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of the Moravian Church, stated that Holy Communion is the "most intimate of all connection with the person of the Saviour."[[125]](#cite_note-Knouse2008-132)
The Order of Service for the observance of the Lord's Supper includes a salutation, hymns, the [right hand of fellowship](/wiki/Right_hand_of_fellowship "Right hand of fellowship"), prayer, consecration of the elements, distribution of the elements, partaking of the elements, and a benediction.[[126]](#cite_note-Moravian2010-133) Moravian Christians traditionally practice footwashing before partaking in the Lord's Supper, although in certain Moravian congregations, this rite is observed chiefly on [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday").[[127]](#cite_note-Vos2009-134)[[128]](#cite_note-Benham-135)

#### Anglican

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Anglican")]

Main article: [Eucharist in Anglicanism](/wiki/Eucharist_in_Anglicanism "Eucharist in Anglicanism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Holy_Communion%2C_Owen_Jones.png/250px-Holy_Communion%2C_Owen_Jones.png)](/wiki/File:Holy_Communion,_Owen_Jones.png)

Illuminated title of "The Holy Communion" from the [1845 illustrated *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1845_illuminated_version) "Book of Common Prayer (1845 illuminated version)")

Anglican theology on the matter of the Eucharist is nuanced. The Eucharist is neither wholly a matter of transubstantiation nor simply devotional and [memorialist](/wiki/Memorialism "Memorialism") in orientation. The Anglican churches do not adhere to the belief that the Lord's Supper is merely a devotional reflection on Christ's death. For some Anglicans, Christ is spiritually present in the fullness of his person in the Eucharist.

The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") itself has repeatedly refused to make official any definition of "the presence of Christ". Church authorities prefer to leave it a mystery while proclaiming the consecrated bread and wine to be "spiritual food" of "Christ's Most Precious Body and Blood"; the bread and wine are an "outward sign of an inner grace".[[129]](#cite_note-136): 859 The words of administration at communion allow for real presence or for a real but spiritual presence (Calvinist receptionism and virtualism). This concept was congenial to most Anglicans well into the 19th century.[[130]](#cite_note-137) From the 1840s, the Tractarians reintroduced the idea of "the real presence" to suggest a corporeal presence, which could be done since the language of the BCP rite referred to the body and blood of Christ without details as well as referring to these as spiritual food at other places in the text. Both are found in the Latin and other rites, but in the former, a definite interpretation as corporeal is applied.

Both [receptionism](/wiki/Receptionism "Receptionism") and virtualism assert the real presence. The former places emphasis on the recipient and the latter states "the presence" is confected by the power of the Holy Spirit but not in Christ's natural body. His presence is objective and does not depend on its existence from the faith of the recipient. The liturgy petitions that elements "be" rather than "become" the body and blood of Christ leaving aside any theory of a change in the natural elements: bread and wine are the outer reality and "the presence" is the inner invisible except as perceived in faith.[[131]](#cite_note-138): 314–324

In 1789, the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") in the United States restored explicit language that the Eucharist is an [oblation](/wiki/Oblation "Oblation") (sacrifice) to God. Subsequent revisions of the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")* by member churches of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") have done likewise (the Church of England did so in the [proposed 1928 prayer book](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1928,_England) "Book of Common Prayer (1928, England)")).[[132]](#cite_note-139): 318–324

The so-called "[Black Rubric](/wiki/Black_Rubric "Black Rubric")" in the [1552 prayer book](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1552) "Book of Common Prayer (1552)"), which allowed kneeling when receiving Holy Communion was omitted in the [1559 edition](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1559) "Book of Common Prayer (1559)") at Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I")'s insistence. It was reinstated in the [1662 prayer book](/wiki/1662_prayer_book "1662 prayer book"), modified to deny any corporal presence of Christ's natural flesh and blood, which are in Heaven and not here.[[133]](#cite_note-140)[[134]](#cite_note-141)[[135]](#cite_note-142)

#### Baptists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Baptists")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Communion_Baptist.jpg/330px-Communion_Baptist.jpg)](/wiki/File:Communion_Baptist.jpg)

The serving of elements individually, to be taken in unison, is common among Baptists.

The bread and "fruit of the vine" indicated in Matthew, Mark and Luke as the elements of the Lord's Supper[[136]](#cite_note-143) are interpreted by many [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") as unleavened bread (although leavened bread is often used) and, in line with the historical stance of some Baptist groups (since the mid-19th century) against partaking of alcoholic beverages, [grape juice](/wiki/Grape_juice "Grape juice"), which they commonly refer to simply as "the Cup".[[137]](#cite_note-144) The unleavened bread also underscores the symbolic belief attributed to Christ's breaking the bread and saying that it was his body.

Some Baptists, such as certain [Independent Baptist](/wiki/Independent_Baptist "Independent Baptist") congregations, consider the Communion to be primarily an act of remembrance of Christ's atonement, and a time of renewal of personal commitment ([memorialism](/wiki/Memorialism "Memorialism")),[[138]](#cite_note-145)[[139]](#cite_note-146) while others, such as [Reformed Baptists](/wiki/Reformed_Baptists "Reformed Baptists") (Calvinistic Baptists) affirm the [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") doctrine of a [pneumatic presence](/wiki/Eucharistic_theology#Pneumatic_presence "Eucharistic theology"),[[140]](#cite_note-Wax2007-147) which is expressed in confessions of faith such as the [Second London Baptist Confession](/wiki/1689_Baptist_Confession_of_Faith "1689 Baptist Confession of Faith"), specifically in Chapter 30, Articles 3 and 7. As such, those within the [Founders movement](/wiki/Founders_Ministries "Founders Ministries") (a Calvinistic movement among the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention")) adhere to the real spiritual presence.[[140]](#cite_note-Wax2007-147) Apart from the Reformed Baptists, there are certain [General Baptists](/wiki/General_Baptists "General Baptists") adhere to the view of the real spiritual presence of Christ in the Lord's Supper, which was taught in Helwys Confession (1611).[[141]](#cite_note-RR1999-148)

Communion practices and frequency vary among congregations. A typical practice among some Baptists is to have small cups of juice and plates of broken bread distributed to the seated congregation. In other congregations, especially the more traditional ones, communicants may proceed to the altar or communion table to receive the elements, then return to their pews. A widely accepted practice is for all to receive and hold the elements until everyone is served, then consume the bread and cup in unison. Usually, hymns are performed and Scripture such as the precise verses of Jesus speaking at the Last Supper is read during the receiving of the elements.

Some Baptist churches are closed-Communionists (even requiring full membership in the local church congregation before partaking), with others being partially or fully open-Communionists. Adults and children in attendance who have not received baptism are expected to not participate.

#### Lutheran

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Lutheran")]

Main article: [Eucharist in Lutheranism](/wiki/Eucharist_in_Lutheranism "Eucharist in Lutheranism")

Further information: [Mass (liturgy) § Lutheranism](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Lutheranism "Mass (liturgy)"), and [Divine Service (Lutheran)](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Lutheran) "Divine Service (Lutheran)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Elevation_nattvardskalk_Hedvig_Eleonora%2C_versus_populum.jpg/250px-Elevation_nattvardskalk_Hedvig_Eleonora%2C_versus_populum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elevation_nattvardskalk_Hedvig_Eleonora,_versus_populum.jpg)

The [elevation](/wiki/Elevation_(liturgy) "Elevation (liturgy)") of the [chalice](/wiki/Chalice "Chalice") during the [Evangelical-Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") celebration of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy)#Lutheranism "Mass (liturgy)") at [Hedvig Eleonora Church](/wiki/Hedvig_Eleonora_Church "Hedvig Eleonora Church") in Stockholm, Sweden

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Nattvard_i_Maria_Magdalena_kyrka.jpg/250px-Nattvard_i_Maria_Magdalena_kyrka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nattvard_i_Maria_Magdalena_kyrka.jpg)

The faithful receiving the Eucharist kneeling at the [communion rails](/wiki/Communion_rail "Communion rail") during an Evangelical-Lutheran [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday") Mass in the [Church of Saint Mary Magdalene](/wiki/Maria_Magdalena_Church "Maria Magdalena Church"), Stockholm, Sweden.

[Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") believe that the body and blood of Christ are "truly and substantially present in, with, and under the forms" of the consecrated bread and wine (the elements), so that communicants eat and drink the body and blood of Christ himself as well as the bread and wine in the Eucharistic [sacrament](/wiki/Lutheran_sacraments "Lutheran sacraments").[[142]](#cite_note-149) The Lutheran doctrine of the Real Presence is more accurately and formally known as the "[sacramental union](/wiki/Sacramental_union "Sacramental union")".[[143]](#cite_note-MattoxRoeber-150)[[144]](#cite_note-151) Others have erroneously called this [consubstantiation](/wiki/Consubstantiation "Consubstantiation"), a [Lollardist](/wiki/Lollardist "Lollardist") doctrine, though this term is specifically rejected by Lutheran churches and theologians since it creates confusion about the actual doctrine and subjects the doctrine to the control of a non-biblical philosophical concept in the same manner as, in their view, does the term "[transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation")".[[145]](#cite_note-152)

In the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"), the Eucharist is celebrated at least every Sunday, with some churches offering it daily, as with monasteries and convents.[[146]](#cite_note-153) There is in Lutheran congregations, a movement to celebrate Eucharist weekly, though it was historically common for congregations to celebrate the Eucharist monthly or even quarterly.[[147]](#cite_note-154)[[148]](#cite_note-155) Even in congregations where Eucharist is offered weekly, there is not a requirement that every church service be a Eucharistic service, nor that all members of a congregation must receive it weekly.[[149]](#cite_note-156)

#### Open Brethren and Exclusive Brethren

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Open Brethren and Exclusive Brethren")]

Among [Open](/wiki/Open_Brethren "Open Brethren") assemblies, also termed [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren"), the Eucharist is more commonly called the Breaking of Bread or the Lord's Supper. They believe it is only a symbolic reenactment of the Last Supper and a memorial "to remind believers of his body given and his blood shed for their salvation".[[9]](#cite_note-Bristow2007-9)[[150]](#cite_note-Gibson-157) and is central to the worship of both individual and assembly.[[151]](#cite_note-Darby-158): 375 In principle, the service is open to all [baptized](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") Christians, but an individual's eligibility to participate depends on the views of each particular assembly. The service takes the form of non-liturgical, open worship with all male participants allowed to pray audibly and select hymns or readings. The breaking of bread itself typically consists of one leavened loaf, which is prayed over and broken by a participant in the meeting[[152]](#cite_note-159): 279–281 and then shared around. The wine is poured from a single container into one or several vessels, and these are again shared around.[[153]](#cite_note-160): 375[[154]](#cite_note-161)

The [Exclusive Brethren](/wiki/Exclusive_Brethren "Exclusive Brethren") follow a similar practice to the [Open Brethren](/wiki/Open_Brethren "Open Brethren"). They also call the Eucharist the Breaking of Bread or the Lord's Supper.[[151]](#cite_note-Darby-158)

#### Reformed (Continental Reformed, Presbyterian and Congregationalist)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Reformed (Continental Reformed, Presbyterian and Congregationalist)")]

Main article: [Lord's Supper in Reformed theology](/wiki/Lord%27s_Supper_in_Reformed_theology "Lord's Supper in Reformed theology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/South_Leith_communion_token_reverse.jpg/250px-South_Leith_communion_token_reverse.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_Leith_communion_token_reverse.jpg)

Many Presbyterian churches historically used [communion tokens](/wiki/Communion_token "Communion token") to provide entrance to the Lord's Supper.

In the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") (which includes the [Continental Reformed Churches](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Church "Continental Reformed Church"), the [Presbyterian Churches](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church "Presbyterian Church"), and the [Congregationalist Churches](/wiki/Congregationalist_Church "Congregationalist Church")), the Eucharist is variously administered. The Calvinist view of the Sacrament sees a real presence of Christ in the supper which differs both from the objective ontological presence of the Catholic view, and from the real absence of Christ and the mental recollection of the memorialism of the Zwinglians[[155]](#cite_note-162): 189 and their successors.

The bread and wine become the means by which the believer has real communion with Christ in his death and Christ's body and blood are present to the faith of the believer as really as the bread and wine are present to their senses but this presence is "spiritual", that is the work of the Holy Spirit.[[156]](#cite_note-163) There is no standard frequency; John Calvin desired communion “at least weekly" (*[Institutes](/wiki/Institutes_of_the_Christian_Religion "Institutes of the Christian Religion")*, 4.17.43) but the city council only approved monthly, and monthly celebration has become the most common practice in Reformed churches today.

Many, on the other hand, follow [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox") in celebration of the Lord's supper on a quarterly basis, to give proper time for reflection and inward consideration of one's own state and sin. Recently, Presbyterian and Reformed Churches have been considering whether to restore more frequent communion, including weekly communion in more churches, considering that infrequent communion was derived from a memorialist view of the Lord's Supper, rather than Calvin's view of the sacrament as a means of grace.[[157]](#cite_note-164) Among [Dutch Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism") advocates of this practice are [United Reformed Churches in North America](/wiki/United_Reformed_Churches_in_North_America "United Reformed Churches in North America") (URCNA) ministers [R. Scott Clark](/wiki/R._Scott_Clark "R. Scott Clark"),[[158]](#cite_note-165) [W. Robert Godfrey](/wiki/W._Robert_Godfrey "W. Robert Godfrey"),[[159]](#cite_note-166) [Michael Horton](/wiki/Michael_Horton_(theologian) "Michael Horton (theologian)"),[[160]](#cite_note-167) Daniel R. Hyde,[[161]](#cite_note-168) and Kim Riddlebarger.[[162]](#cite_note-169) Some churches use bread without any [raising agent](/wiki/Leavening_agent "Leavening agent") (either [yeast](/wiki/Yeast "Yeast") or another [leaven](/wiki/Leaven "Leaven")) in view of the use of [unleavened bread](/wiki/Matzah "Matzah") at [Jewish Passover meals](/wiki/Passover_seder "Passover seder"), while others use any bread available.

[![A photo of communion tray used to serve grape juice in a church affiliated with the United Church of Canada.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Communion_Tray.jpg/250px-Communion_Tray.jpg)](/wiki/File:Communion_Tray.jpg)

A communion tray used to serve grape juice in the [United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada").

The [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), for instance, prescribes "bread common to the culture". Harking back to the [regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship"), the Reformed tradition had long eschewed coming forward to receive communion, preferring to have the elements distributed throughout the congregation by the presbyters (elders) more in the style of a shared meal. Over the last half a century it is much more common in Presbyterian churches to have Holy Communion monthly or on a weekly basis. It is also becoming common to receive the elements by [intinction](/wiki/Intinction "Intinction") (receiving a piece of consecrated bread or wafer, dipping it in the blessed wine, and consuming it). Wine and grape juice are both used, depending on the congregation.[[163]](#cite_note-170)[[164]](#cite_note-171) Most Reformed churches practice "open communion", i.e., all believers who are united to a church of like faith and practice, and who are not living in sin, would be allowed to join in the Sacrament.

#### Methodist

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Methodist")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a4/Methodistcommunion6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Methodistcommunion6.jpg)

A United Methodist minister consecrating the elements

The British *Catechism for the use of the people called Methodists* states that, "[in the Eucharist] Jesus Christ is [present](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist") with his worshipping people and gives himself to them as their Lord and Saviour".[[165]](#cite_note-Methcat-172) Methodist theology of this sacrament is reflected in one of the fathers of the movement, [Charles Wesley](/wiki/Charles_Wesley "Charles Wesley"), who wrote a Eucharistic hymn with the following stanza:[[166]](#cite_note-AbrahamWatson2013-173)

We need not now go up to Heaven,  
To bring the long sought Saviour down;  
Thou art to all already given,  
Thou dost e'en now Thy banquet crown:  
To every faithful soul appear,  
And show Thy real presence here!

Reflecting [Wesleyan covenant theology](/wiki/Wesleyan_covenant_theology "Wesleyan covenant theology"), Methodists also believe that the Lord's Supper is a sign and seal of the [covenant of grace](/wiki/Covenant_of_grace "Covenant of grace").[[167]](#cite_note-Crowther1815-174)[[168]](#cite_note-Watson1852-175)

In many [Methodist denominations](/wiki/List_of_Methodist_denominations "List of Methodist denominations"), non-alcoholic wine (grape juice) is used, so as to include those who do not take alcohol for any reason, as well as a commitment to the Church's historical support of [temperance](/wiki/Temperance_movement "Temperance movement").[[169]](#cite_note-176)[[170]](#cite_note-177) Variations of the [Eucharistic Prayer](/wiki/Eucharistic_Prayer "Eucharistic Prayer") are provided for various occasions, including communion of the sick and brief forms for occasions that call for greater brevity. Though the ritual is standardized, there is great variation amongst Methodist churches, from typically high-church to low-church, in the enactment and style of celebration. Methodist clergy are not required to be vested when celebrating the Eucharist.

[John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley"), a founder of Methodism, said that it was the duty of Christians to receive the sacrament as often as possible. Methodists in the United States are encouraged to celebrate the Eucharist every Sunday, though it is typically celebrated on the first Sunday of each month, while a few go as long as celebrating quarterly (a tradition dating back to the days of [circuit riders](/wiki/Circuit_rider_(religious) "Circuit rider (religious)") that served multiple churches). Communicants may receive standing, kneeling, or while seated. Gaining more wide acceptance is the practice of receiving by intinction (receiving a piece of consecrated bread or wafer, dipping it in the blessed wine, and consuming it). The most common alternative to intinction is for the communicants to receive the consecrated juice using small, individual, specially made glass or plastic cups known as [communion cups](/wiki/Communion_cup "Communion cup").[[171]](#cite_note-178) The [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church") practices open communion (which it describes as an "[open table](/wiki/Open_table "Open table")"), inviting "all who intend a Christian life, together with their children" to receive the eucharistic elements.[[172]](#cite_note-179) *The Doctrines and Discipline of the Methodist Church* specifies, on days during which Holy Communion is celebrated, that "Upon entering the church let the communicants bow in prayer and in the spirit of prayer and meditation approach the Blessed Sacrament."[[173]](#cite_note-180)

#### Nondenominational Christians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Nondenominational Christians")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Lord%27s_cup_and_Bread.JPG/250px-Lord%27s_cup_and_Bread.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lord%27s_cup_and_Bread.JPG)

Communion elements: [matzo](/wiki/Matzah "Matzah") is sometimes used for bread, emphasising the "re-creation" of the Last Supper.

Many non-denominational Christians, including the [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ"), receive communion every Sunday. Others, including [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") churches such as the [Church of God](/wiki/Church_of_God_(Anderson,_Indiana) "Church of God (Anderson, Indiana)") and [Calvary Chapel](/wiki/Calvary_Chapel "Calvary Chapel"), typically receive communion on a monthly or periodic basis. Non-denominational churches are often [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomous "Autonomous"), with governance and practices determined by individual congregations rather than by a central authority.[[174]](#cite_note-181)

Some [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ"), among others, use [grape juice](/wiki/Grape_juice "Grape juice") and unleavened wafers or unleavened bread and practice open communion.

### Syriac Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Syriac Christianity")]

#### Edessan Rite (Church of the East)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Edessan Rite (Church of the East)")]

Main article: [Holy Qurbana](/wiki/Holy_Qurbana "Holy Qurbana")

**Holy Qurbana** or **Qurbana Qaddisha**, the "Holy Offering" or "Holy Sacrifice", refers to the Eucharist as celebrated according to the [East Syriac Christianity](/wiki/East_Syrian_Rite "East Syrian Rite"). The main [Anaphora](/wiki/Anaphora_(liturgy) "Anaphora (liturgy)") of the East Syrian tradition is the [Holy Qurbana of Addai and Mari](/wiki/Holy_Qurbana_of_Addai_and_Mari "Holy Qurbana of Addai and Mari").

#### Syro-Antiochene Rite (West Syriac)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Syro-Antiochene Rite (West Syriac)")]

Main article: [Holy Qurobo](/wiki/Holy_Qurobo "Holy Qurobo")

**Holy Qurobo** or **Qurobo Qadisho** refers to the Eucharist as celebrated in the [West Syrian traditions](/wiki/West_Syrian_Rite "West Syrian Rite") of [Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity"), while that of the West Syrian tradition is the [Liturgy of Saint James](/wiki/Liturgy_of_Saint_James "Liturgy of Saint James").

Both are extremely old, going back at least to the third century, and are the oldest extant liturgies continually in use.

### Restorationism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Restorationism")]

#### Irvingian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Irvingian")]

In the [Irvingian Churches](/wiki/Irvingian_Church "Irvingian Church"), Holy Communion, along with Holy Baptism and Holy Sealing, is one of the three [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments "Sacraments").[[175]](#cite_note-Whalen1981-182)[[176]](#cite_note-Nomos1992-183) It is the focus of the Divine Service in the liturgies of Irvingism.[[177]](#cite_note-184)

[Edward Irving](/wiki/Edward_Irving "Edward Irving"), who founded the Irvingian Churches, such as the [New Apostolic Church](/wiki/New_Apostolic_Church "New Apostolic Church"), taught the [real presence of Christ in the Eucharist](/wiki/Real_presence_of_Christ_in_the_Eucharist "Real presence of Christ in the Eucharist"), emphasizing "the *humiliated* humanity of Christ in the Lord's Supper."[[178]](#cite_note-Lee2018-185)[[179]](#cite_note-Bennett2014-186)[[180]](#cite_note-NAC2020-187) Additionally, the Irvingian Churches affirm the "real presence of the sacrifice of Jesus Christ in Holy Communion":[[180]](#cite_note-NAC2020-187)

> Jesus Christ is in the midst of the congregation as the crucified, risen, and returning Lord. Thus His once-brought sacrifice is also present in that its effect grants the individual access to salvation. In this way, the celebration of Holy Communion causes the partakers to repeatedly envision the sacrificial death of the Lord, which enables them to proclaim it with conviction (1 Corinthians 11: 26).[[181]](#cite_note-188)

In the Irvingian tradition of Restorationist Christianity, [consubstantiation](/wiki/Consubstantiation "Consubstantiation") is taught as the explanation of how the real presence is effected in the liturgy.[[182]](#cite_note-NAC-189)

#### Seventh-day Adventists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Seventh-day Adventists")]

In the [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") the Holy Communion service customarily is celebrated once per quarter. The service includes the ordinance of [footwashing](/wiki/Footwashing "Footwashing") and the Lord's Supper. Unleavened bread and unfermented (non-alcoholic) grape juice is used. [Open communion](/wiki/Open_communion "Open communion") is practised: all who have committed their lives to the Saviour may participate. The communion service must be conducted by an ordained pastor, minister or church elder.[[183]](#cite_note-190)[[184]](#cite_note-191)

#### Jehovah's Witnesses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Jehovah's Witnesses")]

Main article: [Memorial (Jehovah's Witnesses)](/wiki/Memorial_(Jehovah%27s_Witnesses) "Memorial (Jehovah's Witnesses)")

[Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") commemorate Jesus' death annually on the evening that corresponds to the Passover,[[185]](#cite_note-192) [Nisan 14](/wiki/Quartodeciman "Quartodeciman"), according to the ancient [Jewish calendar](/wiki/Jewish_calendar "Jewish calendar").[[186]](#cite_note-193) They generally refer to the observance as "the Lord's Evening Meal" or the "Memorial of Christ's Death". They believe the event is the only annual religious observance commanded for Christians in the Bible.[[187]](#cite_note-194)

Of those who attend the Memorial, a small minority worldwide partake of the wine and unleavened bread. Jehovah's Witnesses believe that only [144,000](/wiki/144,000#Jehovah's_Witnesses "144,000") people will go to heaven, to serve as under-priests and co-rulers with [Christ the King](/wiki/Christ_the_King "Christ the King") in [God's Kingdom](/wiki/God%27s_Kingdom "God's Kingdom"). They are referred to as the "anointed" class. They believe that the baptized "[other sheep](/wiki/Other_sheep "Other sheep")" also benefit from the ransom sacrifice, and are respectful observers and viewers of the Lord's Supper, but they hope to obtain everlasting life in Paradise restored on earth.[[188]](#cite_note-jwbh-195)

The Memorial, held after sundown, includes a sermon on the meaning and importance of the celebration and gathering, and includes the circulation of unadulterated red wine and unleavened bread (matzo). Jehovah's Witnesses believe that the bread represents Jesus' perfect body which he gave on behalf of mankind, and that the wine represents his perfect blood which he shed to redeem fallen man from inherited sin and death. The wine and the bread (sometimes referred to as "emblems") are viewed as symbolic and commemorative; the Witnesses do not believe in [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") or [consubstantiation](/wiki/Consubstantiation "Consubstantiation").[[188]](#cite_note-jwbh-195)[[189]](#cite_note-196)

#### Latter-day Saints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Latter-day Saints")]

Main article: [Sacrament (LDS Church)](/wiki/Sacrament_(LDS_Church) "Sacrament (LDS Church)")

In [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"), the "Holy Sacrament of the Lord's Supper",[[26]](#cite_note-LDS_Sacrament-26) more simply referred to as the Sacrament, is administered every Sunday (except General Conference or other special Sunday meetings) in each [Latter-Day Saint Ward](/wiki/Ward_(LDS_Church) "Ward (LDS Church)") or branch worldwide at the beginning of [Sacrament meeting](/wiki/Sacrament_meeting "Sacrament meeting"). The Sacrament, which consists of both ordinary bread and water (rather than wine or grape juice), is prepared by [priesthood](/wiki/Priesthood_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Priesthood (Latter Day Saints)") holders prior to the beginning of the meeting.
