At the beginning of the Sacrament, [priests](/wiki/Priest_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Priest (Latter Day Saints)") say specific prayers to bless the bread and water.[[190]](#cite_note-197) The Sacrament is passed row-by-row to the congregation by priesthood holders (typically [deacons](/wiki/Deacon_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Deacon (Latter Day Saints)")).[[191]](#cite_note-lds._org-198)

The prayer recited for the bread and the water is found in the [Book of Mormon](/wiki/Book_of_Mormon "Book of Mormon")[[192]](#cite_note-199)[[193]](#cite_note-200) and Doctrine and Covenants. The prayer contains the above essentials given by Jesus: "Always remember him, and keep his commandments […] that they may always have his Spirit to be with them." (Moroni, 4:3.)[[194]](#cite_note-201)

## Non-observing denominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Non-observing denominations")]

### Salvation Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Salvation Army")]

While the [Salvation Army](/wiki/Salvation_Army "Salvation Army") does not reject the Eucharistic practices of other churches or deny that their members truly receive grace through this sacrament, it does not practice the sacraments of Communion or [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"). This is because they believe that these are unnecessary for the living of a Christian life, and because in the opinion of Salvation Army founders William and Catherine Booth, the sacrament placed too much stress on outward ritual and too little on inward spiritual conversion.[[195]](#cite_note-202)

### Quakers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Quakers")]

Emphasizing the inward spiritual experience of their adherents over any outward ritual, the Religious Society of Friends ([Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers")) historically do not baptize or observe physical Communion.[[196]](#cite_note-203) [George Fox](/wiki/George_Fox "George Fox"), founder of the society, emphasized a personal relationship with God. Quakers associated with the [Friends General Conference](/wiki/Friends_General_Conference "Friends General Conference"), [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Friends "Conservative Friends"), [Central Yearly Meeting of Friends](/wiki/Central_Yearly_Meeting_of_Friends "Central Yearly Meeting of Friends"), and the [Friends United Meeting](/wiki/Friends_United_Meeting "Friends United Meeting")[[197]](#cite_note-204) continue to not observe the practice. Some [Evangelical Quakers](/wiki/Evangelical_Friends_Church_International "Evangelical Friends Church International"), however, do sometimes offer voluntary outward rituals, although most continue to emphasize an inward spiritual experience.[[198]](#cite_note-205)

### Christian Scientists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Christian Scientists")]

Although the early [Church of Christ, Scientist](/wiki/Church_of_Christ,_Scientist "Church of Christ, Scientist") observed Communion, founder [Mary Baker Eddy](/wiki/Mary_Baker_Eddy "Mary Baker Eddy") eventually discouraged the physical ritual as she believed it distracted from the true spiritual nature of the sacrament. As such, [Christian Scientists](/wiki/Christian_Scientist "Christian Scientist") do not observe physical communion with bread and wine, but spiritual communion at two special Sunday services each year by "uniting together with Christ in silent prayer and on bended knee".[[199]](#cite_note-206)

### Shakers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Shakers")]

The United Society of Believers (commonly known as [Shakers](/wiki/Shakers "Shakers")) do not take communion, instead viewing every meal as a Eucharistic feast.[[200]](#cite_note-:0-207)

## Practice and customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Practice and customs")]

### Open and closed communion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Open and closed communion")]

Main articles: [Open communion](/wiki/Open_communion "Open communion"), [Closed communion](/wiki/Closed_communion "Closed communion"), and [Full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Eucharist001.jpg/250px-Eucharist001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eucharist001.jpg)

In the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), the administration of the Eucharist to children requires that they have sufficient knowledge and careful preparation to receive the body of Christ with faith and devotion.

[Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") denominations differ in their understanding of whether they may celebrate the Eucharist with those with whom they are not in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion"). The [apologist](/wiki/Apologist "Apologist") [Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr") (c. 150) wrote of the Eucharist "of which no one is allowed to partake but the man who believes that we teach are true, and who has been washed with the washing that is for the remission of sins and unto regeneration, and who is so living as Christ has enjoined."[[201]](#cite_note-208) This was continued in the practice of dismissing the [catechumens](/wiki/Catechumen "Catechumen") (those still undergoing instruction and not yet baptized) before the sacramental part of the liturgy, a custom which has left traces in the expression "[Mass of the Catechumens](/wiki/Mass_of_the_Catechumens "Mass of the Catechumens")" and in the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") exclamation by the deacon or priest, "The doors! The doors!", just before recitation of the Creed.[[202]](#cite_note-209)

Churches such as the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") Churches practice [closed communion](/wiki/Closed_communion "Closed communion") under normal circumstances. However, the Catholic Church allows administration of the Eucharist, at their spontaneous request, to properly disposed members of the eastern churches ([Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox"), [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox") and [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East")) not in full communion with it and of other churches that the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") judges to be sacramentally in the same position as these churches; and in grave and pressing need, such as danger of death, it allows the Eucharist to be administered also to individuals who do not belong to these churches but who share the Catholic Church's faith in the reality of the Eucharist and have no access to a minister of their own community.[[203]](#cite_note-210) Some [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") communities exclude non-members from Communion.

The [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America") (ELCA) practices open communion, provided those who receive are baptized,[[204]](#cite_note-211)[[205]](#cite_note-212) but the [Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod") and the [Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Wisconsin_Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod") (WELS) practice closed communion, excluding non-members and requiring communicants to have been given [catechetical](/wiki/Luther%27s_Small_Catechism "Luther's Small Catechism") instruction.[[206]](#cite_note-213)[[207]](#cite_note-214) The [Evangelical Lutheran Church in Canada](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_Canada "Evangelical Lutheran Church in Canada"), the [Evangelical Church in Germany](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_in_Germany "Evangelical Church in Germany"), the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"), and many other Lutheran churches outside of the U.S. also practice open communion.

Some use the term "close communion" for restriction to members of the same denomination, and "closed communion" for restriction to members of the local congregation alone.

Most [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") communities including [Congregational churches](/wiki/Congregational_churches "Congregational churches"), the [Church of the Nazarene](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nazarene "Church of the Nazarene"), the [Assemblies of God](/wiki/Assemblies_of_God "Assemblies of God"), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), most [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") and [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), and [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ") and other [non-denominational churches](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") practice various forms of [open communion](/wiki/Open_communion "Open communion"). Some churches do not limit it to only members of the congregation, but to any people in attendance (regardless of Christian affiliation) who consider themselves to be Christian. Others require that the communicant be a baptized person, or a member of a church of that denomination or a denomination of "like faith and practice". Some Progressive Christian congregations offer communion to any individual who wishes to commemorate the life and teachings of Christ, regardless of religious affiliation.[[h]](#cite_note-216)

Most Latter-Day Saint churches practice closed communion; one notable exception is the [Community of Christ](/wiki/Community_of_Christ "Community of Christ"), the second-largest denomination in this movement.[[209]](#cite_note-217) While The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (the largest of the LDS denominations) technically practice a closed communion, their official direction to local Church leaders (in Handbook 2, section 20.4.1, last paragraph)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] is as follows: "Although the sacrament is for Church members, the bishopric should not announce that it will be passed to members only, and nothing should be done to prevent nonmembers from partaking of it."[[210]](#cite_note-218)

In the [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church") the Eucharist is only given to those who have come prepared to receive the life-giving body and blood. Therefore, in a manner to worthily receive, believers fast the night before the liturgy, from around 6pm or the conclusion of evening prayer, and remain fasting until they receive Holy Qurbana the next morning. Additionally, members who plan to receive the holy communion have to follow a strict guide of prescribed prayers from the [Shehimo](/wiki/Shehimo "Shehimo"), or the book of common prayers, for the week.[[211]](#cite_note-219)

### Preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Preparation")]

Main article: [Eucharistic discipline](/wiki/Eucharistic_discipline "Eucharistic discipline")

#### Catholic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Catholic")]

The Catholic Church requires its members to receive the [sacrament](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church") of [Penance or Reconciliation](/wiki/Sacrament_of_Penance_(Catholic_Church) "Sacrament of Penance (Catholic Church)") before taking Communion if they are aware of having committed a [mortal sin](/wiki/Mortal_sin "Mortal sin")[[212]](#cite_note-220)[[213]](#cite_note-auto-221) and to prepare by fasting, prayer, and other works of piety.[[213]](#cite_note-auto-221)[[214]](#cite_note-222)

#### Lutheran

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Lutheran")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Dukat_altarbord_Hedvig_Eleonora.jpg/250px-Dukat_altarbord_Hedvig_Eleonora.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dukat_altarbord_Hedvig_Eleonora.jpg)

An [altar](/wiki/Altar "Altar") prepared for communion for hundreds of people during a jubilee [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") at the [Evangelical-Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") [Hedvig Eleonora Parish](/wiki/Hedvig_Eleonora_Parish "Hedvig Eleonora Parish") in Stockholm, Sweden.

The [Small Catechism](/wiki/Luther%27s_Small_Catechism "Luther's Small Catechism"), with regard to the Eucharistic Fast, states: "Fasting and bodily preparation are indeed a fine outward training".[[215]](#cite_note-223)[[216]](#cite_note-Preus2015-224) Though voluntary, the Eucharistic Fast in the [Evangelical-Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") tradition is traditionally kept from midnight until the reception of the Eucharist. Theologically, the "Eucharistic Fast is always in anticipation for the Eucharistic Feast, a reminder of the Last Day when all poverty is abolished, the necessity of fasting has ceased and every prayer answered as all of God's people celebrate the marriage supper of the Lamb."[[217]](#cite_note-Ristau2020-225)

For Evangelical-Lutherans in general, [confession and absolution](/wiki/Confession_(Lutheran_Church) "Confession (Lutheran Church)") are considered proper preparation for receiving the sacrament.[[218]](#cite_note-Book_of_Concord-226) The [Book of Concord](/wiki/Book_of_Concord "Book of Concord"), the compendium of Lutheran dogma, teaches: "Among us…the sacrament is available for all who wish to partake of it after they have been examined and absolved."[[216]](#cite_note-Preus2015-224) Traditionally, Lutheran churches have offered the sacrament of confession on Saturdays so that individuals are able receive the Eucharist on the following day.[[219]](#cite_note-Richard1909-227)[[220]](#cite_note-Kolb2008-228)

#### Eastern Orthodox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox")]

Traditionally, the Eastern Orthodox church has required its members to have observed all church-appointed fasts (most weeks, this will be at least Wednesday and Friday) for the week prior to partaking of communion, and to fast from all food and water from midnight the night before. In addition, Orthodox Christians are to have made a recent confession to their priest (the frequency varying with one's particular priest),[[221]](#cite_note-229) and they must be at peace with all others, meaning that they hold no grudges or anger against anyone.[[222]](#cite_note-How_to_Prepare_for_the_Eucharist-230) In addition, one is expected to attend [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") or the [All-Night Vigil](/wiki/All-Night_Vigil "All-Night Vigil"), if offered, on the night before receiving communion.[[222]](#cite_note-How_to_Prepare_for_the_Eucharist-230) Furthermore, various pre-communion prayers have been composed, which many (but not all) Orthodox churches require or at least strongly encourage members to say privately before coming to the Eucharist.[[223]](#cite_note-231) However, all this will typically vary from priest to priest and jurisdiction to jurisdiction, but abstaining from food and water for several hours beforehand is a fairly universal rule.

#### Other denominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Other denominations")]

A number of churches, including those of the Anglican, Methodist and Reformed traditions, generally reserve a period of time for self-examination and private, silent prayer just before partaking in the Lord's Supper.[[224]](#cite_note-232)[[225]](#cite_note-233)[[226]](#cite_note-234)

### Adoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Adoration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/London_September_2021_Eucharist_Procession_and_Worship.jpg/250px-London_September_2021_Eucharist_Procession_and_Worship.jpg)](/wiki/File:London_September_2021_Eucharist_Procession_and_Worship.jpg)

Worshippers kneel and bow in the street during the Eucharist Procession, London, England.

Further information: [Eucharistic adoration](/wiki/Eucharistic_adoration "Eucharistic adoration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/2e/Eucharistic_Adoration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eucharistic_Adoration.jpg)

The Eucharist displayed in a [monstrance](/wiki/Monstrance "Monstrance"), flanked by candles

Eucharistic adoration is a practice in the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"), Anglo-Catholic and some Lutheran traditions, in which the [Blessed Sacrament](#Other_terms) is exposed to and adored by the faithful. When this exposure and adoration is constant (twenty-four hours a day), it is called "Perpetual Adoration". In a parish, this is usually done by volunteer parishioners; in a [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") or convent, it is done by the resident [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") or [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun"). In the *Exposition of the Blessed Sacrament*, the Eucharist is displayed in a [monstrance](/wiki/Monstrance "Monstrance"), typically placed on an altar, at times with a light focused on it, or with candles flanking it.

### Health issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Health issues")]

#### Gluten

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Gluten")]

Main article: [Coeliac disease § Christian churches and the Eucharist](/wiki/Coeliac_disease#Christian_churches_and_the_Eucharist "Coeliac disease")

The [gluten](/wiki/Gluten "Gluten") in wheat bread is dangerous to people with [celiac disease](/wiki/Celiac_disease "Celiac disease") and other [gluten-related disorders](/wiki/Gluten-related_disorders "Gluten-related disorders"), such as [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity") and [wheat allergy](/wiki/Wheat_allergy "Wheat allergy").[[227]](#cite_note-MulderWanrooijQuotation-235)[[228]](#cite_note-HischenhuberCrevelQuotation-236)[[229]](#cite_note-VoltaCaio2015Quotation-237) For the Catholic Church, this issue was addressed in the 24 July 2003 letter[[230]](#cite_note-238) of the [Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith](/wiki/Congregation_for_the_Doctrine_of_the_Faith "Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith"), which summarized and clarified earlier declarations. The Catholic Church believes that the matter for the Eucharist must be wheaten bread and fermented wine from grapes: it holds that, if the gluten has been entirely removed, the result is not true wheaten bread.[[231]](#cite_note-239) For celiacs, but not generally, it allows low-gluten bread. It also permits Holy Communion to be received under the form of either bread or wine alone, except by a priest who is celebrating Mass without other priests or as principal celebrant.[[232]](#cite_note-240) Many Protestant churches offer communicants gluten-free alternatives to wheaten bread, usually in the form of a rice-based or other gluten-free wafer.[[233]](#cite_note-241)

#### Alcohol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Alcohol")]

See also: [Christian views on alcohol](/wiki/Christian_views_on_alcohol "Christian views on alcohol")

As already indicated, the one exception is in the case of a priest celebrating Mass without other priests or as principal celebrant. The water that in the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") is prescribed to be mixed with the wine must be only a relatively small quantity.[[234]](#cite_note-242) The practice of the [Coptic Church](/wiki/Coptic_Church "Coptic Church") is that the mixture should be two parts wine to one part water.[[235]](#cite_note-243)

Some Protestant churches allow communion in a non-alcoholic form, either normatively or as a pastoral exception. Since the invention of the necessary technology, grape juice which has been [pasteurized](/wiki/Pasteurization "Pasteurization") to stop the fermentation process the juice naturally undergoes and de-alcoholized wine from which most of the alcohol has been removed (between 0.5% and 2% remains) are commonly used, and more rarely water may be offered.[[236]](#cite_note-244) Exclusive use of unfermented grape juice is common in [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") churches, the [United Methodist Church](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church "United Methodist Church"), [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventists "Seventh-day Adventists"), [Christian Churches/Churches of Christ](/wiki/Christian_Churches/Churches_of_Christ "Christian Churches/Churches of Christ"), [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ"), [Church of God (Anderson, Indiana)](/wiki/Church_of_God_(Anderson,_Indiana) "Church of God (Anderson, Indiana)"), some [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Assemblies of God](/wiki/Assemblies_of_God "Assemblies of God"), [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostals "Pentecostals"), [Evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicals "Evangelicals"), the [Christian Missionary Alliance](/wiki/Christian_Missionary_Alliance "Christian Missionary Alliance"), and other American [independent](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") Protestant churches.

For members of the [Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"), water is exclusively used in place of wine.[[237]](#cite_note-245)

#### Transmission of diseases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eucharist&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Transmission of diseases")]

See also: [Impact of the 2019–20 coronavirus pandemic on religion](/wiki/Impact_of_the_2019%E2%80%9320_coronavirus_pandemic_on_religion "Impact of the 2019–20 coronavirus pandemic on religion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Congresso_Clariano_%28Porto_Alegre_%28Brasil%29%2C_20_e_21_set._2012%29_25_Igreja_S%C3%A3o_Francisco_de_Assis_em_Porto_Alegre.JPG/250px-Congresso_Clariano_%28Porto_Alegre_%28Brasil%29%2C_20_e_21_set._2012%29_25_Igreja_S%C3%A3o_Francisco_de_Assis_em_Porto_Alegre.JPG)](/wiki/File:Congresso_Clariano_(Porto_Alegre_(Brasil),_20_e_21_set._2012)_25_Igreja_S%C3%A3o_Francisco_de_Assis_em_Porto_Alegre.JPG)

Catholic communicants in Brazil receive Communion in their hands.

Risk of infectious disease transmission related to use of a common communion cup exists but it is low. No case of transmission of an infectious disease related to a common communion cup has ever been documented. Experimental studies have demonstrated that infectious diseases can be transmitted. The most likely diseases to be transmitted would be common viral illnesses such as the [common cold](/wiki/Common_cold "Common cold"). A study of 681 individuals found that taking communion up to daily from a common cup did not increase the risk of infection beyond that of those who did not attend services at all.[[238]](#cite_note-246)[[239]](#cite_note-247)

In influenza epidemics, some churches suspend the giving wine at communion, for fear of spreading the disease. This is in full accord with Catholic Church belief that communion under the form of bread alone makes it possible to receive all the fruit of Eucharistic grace. However, the same measure has also been taken by churches that normally insist on the importance of receiving [communion under both forms](/wiki/Communion_under_both_kinds "Communion under both kinds"). This was done in 2009 by the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England").[[240]](#cite_note-248)

Some fear contagion through the handling involved in distributing the hosts to the communicants, even if they are placed on the hand rather than on the tongue. Accordingly, some churches use mechanical wafer dispensers or "pillow packs" (communion wafers with wine inside them). While these methods of distributing communion are not generally accepted in Catholic parishes, one parish provides a mechanical dispenser to allow those intending to commune to place in a bowl, without touching them by hand, the hosts for use in the celebration.[[241]](#cite_note-249)
